speaker: interviewer
text: Just give me a little tap. February 6, 1997. Survivor, Dora Drescher, D-R-E-S-C-H-E-R. Maiden name, Gajer, G-A-J-E-R. Interviewer, Irene Squire. We're in Plantation, Florida, USA. Language, English. February 6, 1997. Survivor, Dora Drescher, D-R-E-S-C-H-E-R. Maiden name, Gajer, G-A-J-E-R. Interviewer, Irene Squire. We're in Plantation, Florida, USA. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon, Dora. Begin by telling us your full name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Dora Drescher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dvora Gajer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other names, nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tyszowce, a small town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about the town that you lived in.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town. Was-- the Jews lived like the center of the town. There was a lot of stores and bakeries and groceries. And, you know, it was like a small town in Poland. And every week, there was-- like on Wednesday, there was like a flea market. People-- the peasants would come from all around, from the villages, and they would sell, you know, horses and fruit and produce. And there was no, you know, water. You had to go to a pump in the center of the-- so whoever could afford it would have somebody to bring the water in. They would carry it, you know, on their shoulders, two buckets of water. And who couldn't afford it went and brought it themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish people, would you say, were in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't really know. Maybe [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] 2,000. I really don't know. I don't remember. That's why I say I'm a little--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Esther. My father's name was Hersh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two siblings that died. And later on, my parents had, you know, another two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones that died, his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And a little girl too. Her name was Golda. And after Golda died, my sister was born, Ethel, in 1931.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in 1937, my brother died, I think, in 1935 of brain meningitis. And my parents decided that they want to have a cottage. So my mother was 45 years old when my brother was born. And I'm 12 years older than my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Martin. But then as a baby, his Yiddish name was Motel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your parents do before the war? Was your father-- what was his work?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had like a little store, like a grocery variety. And my mother helped out. And it was-- you know, it was hard to make a living. But everybody survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever help out in the store at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I always helped out. Helped with the children. When my mother had to go to collect money, you know, I would stay with the children. I always helped. I was always responsible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any holidays at home? Did you celebrate the Jewish holidays at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. There's always holidays and Shabbos. And-- my mother used to bake, you know, bread because to buy was expensive. So she would bake breads and cakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have grandparents or family living in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's parents lived in town. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather's name was Yitzhak. And my grandmother's name was Sara, Sura. And I had-- you know, my father had brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: That lived in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That lived in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was a brother, Moshe. Actually, he died here five years ago. And there was a sister, [NON-ENGLISH], the youngest. And one brother was [NON-ENGLISH]. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And he had two sisters. One lived in Warsaw and one lived in a-- in a different town not far from our town. And they all perished, the ones-- the sisters. Actually, my grandparents were in Russia together when, you know-- and they survived the war. And my grandmother even came to Israel after the war, So she's buried in Tel Aviv. When I go to Israel, I go to visit her. And my parents are also in Israel-- buried in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you attend school in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school. Seven grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was school like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- you know, it was-- we went to school with Polish children. They weren't Semites. And the Jewish children were always brighter and smarter than-- and yet if you finished school and you wanted to get a position in the government or something, you couldn't get it because you weren't, you know, non-Jewish. So even though you were smart and everything, but you didn't get a chance because it was anti-Semitic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness any acts of anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, they organized like to make cooperatives. You know, you shouldn't buy by Jews, like before the war in '37-- '37, '38. Right before the war, that's when it started. There was always anti-Semitism. And I think the church was part of it. There's no doubt about it because they always claimed that the Jews killed Jesus. And the people weren't, you know, educated so much, so whatever the church says, this is what they believed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents' business suffer for it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, you know. It wasn't a-- they barely made a living, but we survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any talk in your home about the rise of Hitler, about what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There was always talk of that. And we were scared. And-- you know, the-- sometimes, you know, like the girls in school would say, wait. Wait. Hitler will come and he will fix you. That's what they used to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember hearing anything about Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. No, not really. I heard it after the war. I mean-- but not before. But, I mean, we knew that when Hitler's going to come, there's going to be, like, forced labor, whatever. But nobody anticipated that it's going to be such a, you know, tragedy. That everybody's going to be wiped out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the first signs that you were able to see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, the government was always, you know, anti-Semetic. And I think the government that came into power was like-- I think before what's-his-name died-- Pilsudski was a little bit more liberal, you know? But after he died, I think the more radicals took over. And they were, like, collaborating with the-- you know, with the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually see the Germans come into your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Described that. Describe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an awful feeling. And because of that-- because I was so petrified of them, that we survived, because I refused to say. Because when the Germans came in and then, you know, these motorcycles. And next there's like a passenger next to the motorcycle, with the tanks and-- And then, for some reason, when they made-- Stalin and Hitler made a pact, they-- the Germans went out from our town and the Russians came in. And after that they decide-- they divided Poland in two parts. And the part that we lived was going to be under German rule.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we decided, since I was so petrified-- so my mother wanted me to go away with my father. We should-- like, you know, my father was a young man. It was in '39, so he was 40 years old. He was born 1999. So we-- you know, so my mother wanted us, you know, to go with him. And I said, no way. We're going to go, all of us. And that's the reason we survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russians provided transportation. As they were going out from the part that we lived, they gave wagons and horses. And they took us over on the other side. The brook was the border where one side would be, the Russians and the other side would be the Germans. So we crossed, you know, the border. And we saw-- we saw the bridge, like, blowing up. And the Germans took up position.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to take your things with you? Possessions?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took some things. But what can you take? My brother was two years old, and it was [NON-ENGLISH]. It was raining. It was miserable.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '39. It was like October, '39. The war started in September. And then on Rosh Hashanah, the Germans came in. And then they went out and then the Russians came. And then the Germans were going to come in again. So-- And because I was petrified of the Germans-- I mean, I didn't know about the Holocaust or anything, but the brutality of them. So that's how we survived. I mean, by the grace of God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever witness any acts of brutality?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I didn't see them, no, because we ran away right away. They came in in September and by October we were out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember thinking about whether or not you were going to come back to your home at any point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you always think that you're going to come back. But the problem was that the-- there was no-- when we-- there was a lot of people that did the same thing that we did, with families. You know, our town burned down, so there was no place to live. So that's why most of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did it burn down?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, they bombed it and they burned it down. They torched it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this while you were living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we ran away. We ran away like to, you know, out of town because they were bombing. So we went like to a small village.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember that day? Where were you when that started?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were about 7 kilometers by my-- where my mother was born, in that town. So we came there. And then it was just the whole town. So people had no place to go to-- you know. Most of the Jews lived, like, in the center of the town. And I think even the Polish people, they torched it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you actually living in your home when the bombing started or the torching started?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we weren't. We ran away already. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you run to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards-- about 7 kilometers from our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in my mother's-- in the house where my grandfather used to live, and my-- where my mother was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they still living there, your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My grandparents-- I'm named after my grandmother. But my grandfather died in 1933. But my mother's sister lived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed until we ran away.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was about, I would say, a month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what made you decide to leave again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I was-- I was afraid of the Germans. I was petrified.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they coming in--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we packed up and-- yeah, they were coming in at the same time. So we packed everything up. And, you know-- and it was funny because my sister was eight years old at the time and she didn't feel good. She had a low-grade fever. And we didn't know what was wrong. So, you know, when we left, we came to a town about, I think, eight kilometers. Near--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: By horse and wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose horse and wagons were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- the Russians provided. You know, they gave some drivers and, you know, the guy was taking us. And we stopped at-- there was a shtetl, and all the Jews took all their possessions. It was near the border on the German side, where the Germans were going to be. And they went to the other side. They took everything. So when we came, there was no Jews. Everything was-- And we slept over in a empty house. And when we got up in the morning-- my brother was two years old and he had the measles. The measles came out. He incubated them from my sister. She had the measles, but it didn't show. [LAUGHS] It was funny. And he got up, and he was, like, dipped in a-- in a red thing, or whatever. And we had to cross the border because the Germans were going to come in. So we crossed the border. And we stayed-- there was a little house on the other side-- until he got better.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in that house? Were there people in that house?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think there were other people with us too. I don't remember, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And from there, we went to a-- to a town where my grandmother was born in that town, which was called Wlodzimierz. And my grandparents were there already. So we went there. But when we came there, there was like-- you know, my grandparents, they had a little room. And we were five people. And there was my youngest-- my father's sister. My father's sister was eight years older than I. So when I was 14, she was 22.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was very hard, you know. And there was no housing because there was an influx of people. A lot of people lived in synagogues and, you know, wherever you could. So we decided that we're going-- they were registering to take people to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask you--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we registered-- yeah, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they ask you to become citizens?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is when we came back. This was later on. So when we registered, this was in 1949--

speaker: interviewer
text: No--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939. Right. And then we figured we might as well go. Maybe we'll get work. You know, my father was a young man. But when they took us to Russia, it was, like, very hard. We just couldn't live there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a trip was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was quite a few hours-- quite a few days. We went to-- it was actually-- now,

speaker: interviewee
text: s the Ukraine. It's called Poltava. Near Poltava.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did-- how--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we stayed in a communal farm, like. It's called [NON-ENGLISH], what they used to call, the Russians. And we had to work. And I was a young girl and I had to go, you know, to-- they had like grain storages, you know. And my mother had to work, and I had to go to work. And I was-- you know, I was only-- not even 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived like in a communal-- you know, my-- actually, my father's sister came with us because she thought, you know, whatever. She went with us. So we lived there. But then we decided to go back. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you actually live there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say, I know we came before Passover of 1940. Because the reason I remember, we didn't have any place to stay so we stayed in a shul. You know, there was like a room there. We stayed. A few families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this actually?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- we came back, Lutsk. It's also Volyn, it's called. It's like the part of Poland that the Russians occupied. And when we came back, there was-- you know, we didn't have any money. And I remember my mother and I went to help bake matzos so we could have matzos for, you know, for Passover because we couldn't afford it to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this actually in the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. This was in-- this was in the part of Russia-- in Poland where Russia was occupying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Before.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So now where did yo go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there the Russians came in. They wanted us to become citizens. Actually, they wanted to take Russian passports and we didn't want. We said we want to go back. You know, we don't want to be--

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, they rounded up thousands and thousands of people in cattle cars, and they put us in like 50 in a car. And they shipped us to Siberia for about-- with the KGB. They didn't let us out. Every time, they would open to feed us with porridge. And the KGB would hold the, you know, like the bayonets. And there were 50 people. There was like-- the toilet was like a piece in the cattle wall in the car was cut out. And there was, you know, like-- how do you call it-- wooden-- instead of a bed, there would be like board, you know? Like two, three layers. There was about 50 people in a car.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every station, you would stop, you know, and you would go out. And you would get-- you couldn't cook, so you would get some hot water. Russia had a system that every station would have boiled water. So you would get the boiled water. And some people would even cook, like take some woods like near the-- A lot of people got separated. You know, they went to buy something and the train went away. And a lot of people, you know, got separated. Children from parents and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember being nervous about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was frustrating, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family manage to stay together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we all stayed together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't know. But we figured out that we're going to Siberia because anybody that's not desirable went to Siberia. So we knew it's going to be. But later on, as we came in, you know, deeper into Russia they-- you know, they were more loose already. They didn't lock the doors like this. They would lock the doors outside and they would only open the doors when they would feed you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long a trip was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think about two weeks. Yeah. And people had lice. And, you know, you couldn't bathe. You couldn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would feed you. They would bring like two pails of-- one would be soup and one would be-- it's a grain. It's called, I think, millet. They would bring like with a-- I remember vividly-- like a dark oil, like a yellow oil. It would be like-- you wouldn't eat it, but when you're hungry, you eat anything. And that's what people had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the day when you got off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we got off the train, it was like the end of the train. There was no more-- it was passed Novosibirsk, Tomsk. It was the end of the train. The train didn't go further. So they took us by barge. They loaded us in a barge and they took us, like, to a place. It was called Taiga.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say a barge, you're talking about being on the water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, being on the water.

speaker: interviewer
text: What water?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long a trip was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. I think [NON-ENGLISH]. I think it was a day or two maybe. Yeah. And there was no facilities to go to the bathroom. It was just awful, you know. People would stay one on top of the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the weather like? What time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was summer. It was after Passover. It was-- I think it was in June. And it was very hot when we came to Siberia there. You know, the-- it's like the climate is very hot and there's a lot of little mosquitoes and flies. And when we came into the barracks, they gave you like a room. And the size of the room was like-- you know, with a bed on both sides. It was made out of wood. And there were bed bugs. When we came in, you know, you put in clean sheets that we had with us. And the bugs would be coming out. And the smell! You know, bed bugs have an awful smell. It was just horrifying. And my mother went and she got a job. And I was watching the children. She got a job as a cook because she spoke Russian. My mother spoke Russian because she went to school before the war, you know. There was a-- so she had the language. And she befriended a woman doctor there. And she gave her a job with another woman. And they were cooking for a small hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a paying job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were getting some small-- my father worked cutting down timber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that also a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was paying. A small paying job. Right. And also, there was food, so, you know. So she was able to help other children. There was a lot of children and they were starving. So people would come, like, and put, the-- you know, the walk to the hospital was like from lugs. And people--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does lugs mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wooden, you know, round--

speaker: interviewer
text: Logs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Logs, right. So people would bring in little dishes and she would, like, fill. And my brother used to go there. She would feed him and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school? Were you able to go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what did you do all day when you got off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Watched the children. My-- you know, my brother was three years old at that time. So since my mother worked, I had to take care of the house and clean. And you had to clean. It was like a commandant would come and inspect, you know, if you washed the floor. And you were like in a prison. It was like a camp, but with families, you know. They took people that were single and they put them like in more severe camps. But these were people with families, so they were treated a little bit more lenient.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you all in one room together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father's sister with you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father's sister was-- you know, she went back to the-- they took them separately. Because when we came back from Russia, she went back to her parents, was like Wlodzimierz. And we were in Lutsk. But we all ended up-- you know, my father's brother and another brother and my grandparents, in that neighborhood, you know, in Siberia. And later my grandparents came to live to our-- you know, to our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to pause here to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
